arguments.prompt:
Explore the ModelContextProtocol NuGet package (version 1.2.0) installed in /tmp/sv-25aaefc463ab43348caec5fda34c4f0e/McpStdioServer. I need to understand:

1. What attributes/decorators are used to define MCP tools? (e.g. [McpServerToolType], [McpServerTool], etc.)
2. What attributes/decorators are used to define MCP prompt templates? (e.g. [McpServerPromptType], [McpServerPrompt], etc.)
3. How to set up the server host with stdio transport — what extension methods are available on IHostBuilder or IServiceCollection? (e.g. AddMcpServer, WithStdioTransport, WithTools, WithPrompts, etc.)
4. How logging to stderr is configured to avoid interfering with stdio JSON-RPC.
5. What namespaces are needed.

Look in the NuGet cache (~/.nuget/packages/modelcontextprotocol/) for the DLL and use `dotnet tool` or reflection, or search for XML doc files, README, or source files bundled with the package. Also check if there's a sub-package like ModelContextProtocol.AspNetCore.

Run: find ~/.nuget/packages/modelcontextprotocol* -type f | head -80
Also try: dotnet new list | grep -i mcp
Also look for any README.md or docs bundled in the package.
Also decompile key types: use `dotnet tool install -g dotnet-ildasm 2>/dev/null; ildasm ...` or just list the public types with reflection by writing a small C# script.

Write a small C# script that loads the ModelContextProtocol assembly and prints all public types, their public methods, and attributes. Run it from the project directory.

<member name="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerOptions">
            <summary>
            Provides configuration options for the MCP server.
            </summary>
        </member>
        <member name="P:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerOptions.ServerInfo">
            <summary>
            Gets or sets information about this server implementation, including its name and version.
            </summary>
            <remarks>
            This information is sent to the client during initialization to identify the server.
            It's displayed in client logs and can be used for debugging and compatibility checks.
            </remarks>
        </member>
        <member name="P:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerOptions.Capabilities">
            <summary>
            Gets or sets server capabilities to advertise to the client.
            </summary>
            <remarks>
            These determine which features will be available when a client connects.
            Capabilities can include "tools", "prompts", "resources", "logging", and other
            protocol-specific functionality.
            </remarks>
        </member>
        <member name="P:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerOptions.ProtocolVersion">
            <summary>
            Gets or sets the protocol version supported by this server, using a date-based versioning scheme.
            </summary>
            <remarks>
            The protocol version defines which features and message formats this server supports.
            This uses a date-based versioning scheme in the format "YYYY-MM-DD".
            If <see langword="null"/>, the server will advertise to the client the version requested
            by the client if that version is known to be supported, and otherwise will advertise the latest
            version supported by the server.
            </remarks>
        </member>
        <member name="P:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerOptions.InitializationTimeout">
            <summary>
            Gets or sets a timeout used for the client-server initialization handshake sequence.
            </summary>
            <remarks>
            This timeout determines how long the server will wait for client responses during
            the initialization protocol handshake. If the client doesn't respond within this timeframe,
            the initialization process will be aborted.
            </remarks>
        </member>
        <member name="P:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerOptions.ServerInstructions">
            <summary>
            Gets or sets optional server instructions to send to clients.
            </summary>
            <remarks>
            These instructions are sent to clients during the initialization handshake and provide
            guidance on how to effectively use the server's capabilities. They should focus on
            information that helps models use the server effectively and should not duplicate
            tool, prompt, or resource descriptions already exposed elsewhere.
            Client applications typically use these instructions as system messages for LLM interactions
            to provide context about available functionality.
            </remarks>
        </member>
        <member name="P:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerOptions.ScopeRequests">
            <summary>
            Gets or sets a value that indicates whether to create a new service provider scope for each handled request.
            </summary>
            <value>
            <see langword="true"/> if each invocation of a request handler is invoked within a new service scope.
            The default is <see langword="true"/>.
            </value>
        </member>
        <member name="P:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerOptions.KnownClientInfo">
            <summary>
            Gets or sets preexisting knowledge about the client including its name and version to help support
            stateless Streamable HTTP servers that encode this knowledge in the mcp-session-id header.
            </summary>
            <remarks>
            <para>
            When not specified, this information is sourced from the client's initialize request.
            </para>
            </remarks>
        </member>
        <member name="P:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerOptions.KnownClientCapabilities">
            <summary>
            Gets or sets preexisting knowledge about the client's capabilities to support session migration
            scenarios where the client will not re-send the initialize request.
            </summary>
            <remarks>
            <para>
            When not specified, this information is sourced from the client's initialize request.
            This is typically set during session migration in conjunction with <see cref="P:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerOptions.KnownClientInfo"/>.
            </para>
            </remarks>
        </member>
        <member name="P:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerOptions.Handlers">
            <summary>
            Gets or sets the container of handlers used by the server for processing protocol messages.
            </summary>
        </member>
        <member name="P:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerOptions.Filters">
            <summary>
            Gets or sets the filter collections for MCP server handlers.
            </summary>
            <remarks>

================================================================================
8. LOGGING CONFIGURATION & STDERR HANDLING
================================================================================

Logging Infrastructure:
  - Uses Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.ILoggerFactory
  - StdioServerTransport constructor accepts optional ILoggerFactory parameter
  - Logger factory handles all diagnostic output

Key Classes:
  StdioServerTransport
    Constructor: StdioServerTransport(
        McpServerOptions serverOptions,
        ILoggerFactory loggerFactory)
    
    Or: StdioServerTransport(
        string serverName,
        ILoggerFactory loggerFactory)
    
    Note: loggerFactory parameter is OPTIONAL - can be null for defaults

Stderr Handling for Stdio Transport:
  - Stdio transport communicates via stdin/stdout for JSON-RPC messages
  - Logging output is controlled separately via ILoggerFactory
  - To avoid interfering with JSON-RPC:
    
    1. Configure logging to write to stderr only:
       var loggerFactory = LoggerFactory.Create(builder =>
           builder.AddConsole()
                  .AddConsoleFormatter<SimpleConsoleFormatter, SimpleConsoleFormatterOptions>(options =>
                      options.IncludeScopes = true));
    
    2. Pass loggerFactory to StdioServerTransport
    
    3. Configure client-side stderr handling via:
       StdioClientTransport constructor with StandardErrorLines callback:
         
         var options = new StdioClientTransportOptions
         {
             Command = "dotnet",
             Arguments = "MyServer.dll",
             StandardErrorLines = (line) => 
             {
                 Console.Error.WriteLine($"[Server] {line}");
             }
         };

Best Practices:
  - Logging via ILoggerFactory automatically avoids stdout (reserved for JSON-RPC)
  - Serilog or other structured logging can be configured via LoggerFactory
  - Server instructions specify expected behavior via ServerInstructions property
  - Client specifies logging level via logging/setLevel request

================================================================================
9. REQUIRED NAMESPACES
================================================================================

Core Namespaces:

  using ModelContextProtocol.Server;
    - McpServer, McpServerOptions, McpServerTool, McpServerPrompt
    - [McpServerToolAttribute], [McpServerPromptAttribute]
    - [McpServerToolTypeAttribute], [McpServerPromptTypeAttribute]
    - StdioServerTransport
  
  using ModelContextProtocol.Protocol;
    - JsonRpcMessage, ITransport, Tool, Prompt
  
  using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;
    - IServiceCollection, IServiceProvider, IMcpServerBuilder
    - AddMcpServer, WithStdioServerTransport, WithTools, WithPrompts
  
  using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;
    - ILoggerFactory (for logging configuration)
  
  using System.Text.Json;
    - JsonSerializerOptions (for tool/prompt serialization)

Optional Namespaces:

  using ModelContextProtocol.Client;
    - StdioClientTransport (if building clients)
  
  using ModelContextProtocol.Authentication;
    - For OAuth-based authentication
  
  using Serilog;
  using Serilog.Extensions.Logging;
    - For structured logging integration

================================================================================
10. TYPICAL SERVER SETUP PATTERN
================================================================================

var host = new HostBuilder()
    .ConfigureServices((hostContext, services) =>
    {
        services
            .AddLogging(logging =>
                logging.AddConsole()
                       .SetMinimumLevel(LogLevel.Information))
            .AddMcpServer(options =>
            {
                options.ServerInfo = new Implementation
                {
                    Name = "MyMcpServer",
                    Version = "1.0.0"
                };
            })
            .WithStdioServerTransport()
            .WithTools<ToolsClass>(JsonSerializerDefaults.Web)
            .WithPrompts<PromptsClass>(JsonSerializerDefaults.Web)
            .WithResources();
    })
    .Build();

await host.RunAsync();

8. LOGGING CONFIGURATION & STDERR HANDLING
================================================================================

Logging Infrastructure:
  - Uses Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.ILoggerFactory
  - StdioServerTransport constructor accepts optional ILoggerFactory parameter
  - Logger factory handles all diagnostic output

Key Classes:
  StdioServerTransport
    Constructor: StdioServerTransport(
        McpServerOptions serverOptions,
        ILoggerFactory loggerFactory)
    
    Or: StdioServerTransport(
        string serverName,
        ILoggerFactory loggerFactory)
    
    Note: loggerFactory parameter is OPTIONAL - can be null for defaults

Stderr Handling for Stdio Transport:
  - Stdio transport communicates via stdin/stdout for JSON-RPC messages
  - Logging output is controlled separately via ILoggerFactory
  - To avoid interfering with JSON-RPC:
    
    1. Configure logging to write to stderr only:
       var loggerFactory = LoggerFactory.Create(builder =>
           builder.AddConsole()
                  .AddConsoleFormatter<SimpleConsoleFormatter, SimpleConsoleFormatterOptions>(options =>
                      options.IncludeScopes = true));
    
    2. Pass loggerFactory to StdioServerTransport
    
    3. Configure client-side stderr handling via:
       StdioClientTransport constructor with StandardErrorLines callback:
         
         var options = new StdioClientTransportOptions
         {
             Command = "dotnet",
             Arguments = "MyServer.dll",
             StandardErrorLines = (line) => 
             {
                 Console.Error.WriteLine($"[Server] {line}");
             }
         };

Best Practices:
  - Logging via ILoggerFactory automatically avoids stdout (reserved for JSON-RPC)
  - Serilog or other structured logging can be configured via LoggerFactory
  - Server instructions specify expected behavior via ServerInstructions property
  - Client specifies logging level via logging/setLevel request

var host = new HostBuilder()

            .AddMcpServer(options =>

# MCP C# SDK

[![NuGet version](https://img.shields.io/nuget/v/ModelContextProtocol.svg)](https://www.nuget.org/packages/ModelContextProtocol)

The official C# SDK for the [Model Context Protocol](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/), enabling .NET applications, services, and libraries to implement and interact with MCP clients and servers. Please visit the [API documentation](https://csharp.sdk.modelcontextprotocol.io/api/ModelContextProtocol.html) for more details on available functionality.

## Packages

This SDK consists of three main packages:

- **[ModelContextProtocol.Core](https://www.nuget.org/packages/ModelContextProtocol.Core)** [![NuGet version](https://img.shields.io/nuget/v/ModelContextProtocol.Core.svg)](https://www.nuget.org/packages/ModelContextProtocol.Core) - For projects that only need to use the client or low-level server APIs and want the minimum number of dependencies.

- **[ModelContextProtocol](https://www.nuget.org/packages/ModelContextProtocol)** [![NuGet version](https://img.shields.io/nuget/v/ModelContextProtocol.svg)](https://www.nuget.org/packages/ModelContextProtocol) - The main package with hosting and dependency injection extensions. References `ModelContextProtocol.Core`. This is the right fit for most projects that don't need HTTP server capabilities.

- **[ModelContextProtocol.AspNetCore](https://www.nuget.org/packages/ModelContextProtocol.AspNetCore)** [![NuGet version](https://img.shields.io/nuget/v/ModelContextProtocol.AspNetCore.svg)](https://www.nuget.org/packages/ModelContextProtocol.AspNetCore) - The library for HTTP-based MCP servers. References `ModelContextProtocol`.

## Getting Started

To get started, see the [Getting Started](https://csharp.sdk.modelcontextprotocol.io/concepts/getting-started.html) guide for installation instructions, package-selection guidance, and complete examples for both clients and servers.

You can also browse the [samples](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/csharp-sdk/tree/main/samples) directory and the [API documentation](https://csharp.sdk.modelcontextprotocol.io/api/ModelContextProtocol.html) for more details on available functionality.

## About MCP

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open protocol that standardizes how applications provide context to Large Language Models (LLMs). It enables secure integration between LLMs and various data sources and tools.

For more information about MCP:

- [Official MCP Documentation](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/)
- [MCP C# SDK Documentation](https://csharp.sdk.modelcontextprotocol.io/)
- [Protocol Specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/)
- [GitHub Organization](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol)

Resource Attributes:
  [McpServerResourceAttribute]
    - string Name { get; set; }
    - string Title { get; set; }
    - string IconSource { get; set; }
    - string MimeType { get; set; }
    - string UriTemplate { get; set; }

  [McpServerResourceTypeAttribute]  // Mark containing type

================================================================================
8. LOGGING CONFIGURATION & STDERR HANDLING
================================================================================

Logging Infrastructure:
  - Uses Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.ILoggerFactory
  - StdioServerTransport constructor accepts optional ILoggerFactory parameter
  - Logger factory handles all diagnostic output

Key Classes:
  StdioServerTransport
    Constructor: StdioServerTransport(
        McpServerOptions serverOptions,
        ILoggerFactory loggerFactory)
    
    Or: StdioServerTransport(
        string serverName,
        ILoggerFactory loggerFactory)
    
    Note: loggerFactory parameter is OPTIONAL - can be null for defaults

Stderr Handling for Stdio Transport:
  - Stdio transport communicates via stdin/stdout for JSON-RPC messages
  - Logging output is controlled separately via ILoggerFactory
  - To avoid interfering with JSON-RPC:
    
    1. Configure logging to write to stderr only:
       var loggerFactory = LoggerFactory.Create(builder =>
           builder.AddConsole()
                  .AddConsoleFormatter<SimpleConsoleFormatter, SimpleConsoleFormatterOptions>(options =>
                      options.IncludeScopes = true));
    
    2. Pass loggerFactory to StdioServerTransport
    
    3. Configure client-side stderr handling via:
       StdioClientTransport constructor with StandardErrorLines callback:
         
         var options = new StdioClientTransportOptions
         {
             Command = "dotnet",
             Arguments = "MyServer.dll",
             StandardErrorLines = (line) => 
             {
                 Console.Error.WriteLine($"[Server] {line}");
             }
         };

Best Practices:
  - Logging via ILoggerFactory automatically avoids stdout (reserved for JSON-RPC)
  - Serilog or other structured logging can be configured via LoggerFactory
  - Server instructions specify expected behavior via ServerInstructions property
  - Client specifies logging level via logging/setLevel request

================================================================================
9. REQUIRED NAMESPACES
================================================================================

Core Namespaces:

  using ModelContextProtocol.Server;
    - McpServer, McpServerOptions, McpServerTool, McpServerPrompt
    - [McpServerToolAttribute], [McpServerPromptAttribute]
    - [McpServerToolTypeAttribute], [McpServerPromptTypeAttribute]
    - StdioServerTransport
  
  using ModelContextProtocol.Protocol;
    - JsonRpcMessage, ITransport, Tool, Prompt
  
  using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;
    - IServiceCollection, IServiceProvider, IMcpServerBuilder
    - AddMcpServer, WithStdioServerTransport, WithTools, WithPrompts
  
  using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;
    - ILoggerFactory (for logging configuration)
  
  using System.Text.Json;
    - JsonSerializerOptions (for tool/prompt serialization)

Optional Namespaces:

  using ModelContextProtocol.Client;
    - StdioClientTransport (if building clients)
  
  using ModelContextProtocol.Authentication;
    - For OAuth-based authentication
  
  using Serilog;
  using Serilog.Extensions.Logging;
    - For structured logging integration

================================================================================
10. TYPICAL SERVER SETUP PATTERN
================================================================================

var host = new HostBuilder()
    .ConfigureServices((hostContext, services) =>
    {
        services
            .AddLogging(logging =>
                logging.AddConsole()
                       .SetMinimumLevel(LogLevel.Information))
            .AddMcpServer(options =>
            {
                options.ServerInfo = new Implementation
                {
                    Name = "MyMcpServer",
                    Version = "1.0.0"
                };
            })
            .WithStdioServerTransport()
            .WithTools<ToolsClass>(JsonSerializerDefaults.Web)
            .WithPrompts<PromptsClass>(JsonSerializerDefaults.Web)
            .WithResources();
    })
    .Build();

await host.RunAsync();

================================================================================
11. MUTABLE BUILDER EXTENSION METHODS (Filters & Handlers)
================================================================================

Message Filters:
  IMcpServerBuilder WithMessageFilters(
      this IMcpServerBuilder builder,
      Action<IMcpMessageFilterBuilder> configure)
  
  IMcpMessageFilterBuilder AddIncomingFilter(
      this IMcpMessageFilterBuilder builder,
      McpMessageFilter filter)
  
  IMcpMessageFilterBuilder AddOutgoingFilter(
      this IMcpMessageFilterBuilder builder,
      McpMessageFilter filter)

Request Filters (per-request type):
  IMcpServerBuilder WithRequestFilters(
      this IMcpServerBuilder builder,
      Action<IMcpRequestFilterBuilder> configure)
  
  // Available for each request type:
  IMcpRequestFilterBuilder AddListToolsFilter(...)
  IMcpRequestFilterBuilder AddCallToolFilter(...)
  IMcpRequestFilterBuilder AddListPromptsFilter(...)
  IMcpRequestFilterBuilder AddGetPromptFilter(...)
  IMcpRequestFilterBuilder AddListResourcesFilter(...)
  IMcpRequestFilterBuilder AddReadResourceFilter(...)
  IMcpRequestFilterBuilder AddListResourceTemplatesFilter(...)
  IMcpRequestFilterBuilder AddSubscribeToResourcesFilter(...)
  IMcpRequestFilterBuilder AddUnsubscribeFromResourcesFilter(...)
  IMcpRequestFilterBuilder AddSetLoggingLevelFilter(...)
  IMcpRequestFilterBuilder AddCompleteFilter(...)

Request Handlers:
  IMcpServerBuilder WithListToolsHandler(...)
  IMcpServerBuilder WithCallToolHandler(...)
  IMcpServerBuilder WithListPromptsHandler(...)
  IMcpServerBuilder WithGetPromptHandler(...)
  IMcpServerBuilder WithListResourcesHandler(...)

  IMcpServerBuilder WithSubscribeToResourcesHandler(...)
  IMcpServerBuilder WithUnsubscribeFromResourcesHandler(...)
  IMcpServerBuilder WithSetLoggingLevelHandler(...)
  IMcpServerBuilder WithCompleteHandler(...)
